# SENPAI Research Ideas — 2026-06-01 10:35Z

**Exhausted axes** (do NOT repeat): all calibration variants (7-fold null), K-axis under Gaussian,

## H-A: PCGrad / CAGrad Multi-Task Gradient Surgery on WSS vs Pressure

### What it is

During the backward pass, project the WSS-channel gradient onto the orthogonal
complement of the VP+SP gradient (PCGrad) or find a common descent direction that
minimizes the worst-case per-task loss increase (CAGrad). This surgically removes
gradient conflict between the two task families without changing architecture or data.

### Why it might help

The WSS_z residual is structural (tau-z-cal-saturated finding). One plausible cause
is that the VP and SP gradients, which are numerically larger and lower-frequency
(pressure fields are smoother), suppress or corrupt the WSS_z gradient during
joint training. A standard multi-task weighted loss sum cannot resolve this because
it does not touch gradient directions, only magnitudes. PCGrad/CAGrad work in gradient
space and can recover per-task gradient vectors that would otherwise be silenced.

Evidence from adjacent domains: PCGrad (NeurIPS 2020) showed 1.5–4.0% improvement
on CelebA multi-task, MTL-Survey-2023 reports consistent improvement on heterogeneous
task families; CAGrad (NeurIPS 2021) is stronger when tasks are strongly conflicting
rather than merely orthogonal.

### Key papers

- PCGrad: "Gradient Surgery for Multi-Task Learning" (Yu et al., NeurIPS 2020)
  https://arxiv.org/abs/2001.06782
- CAGrad: "Conflict-Averse Gradient Descent for Multi-task Learning" (Liu et al., NeurIPS 2021)
  https://arxiv.org/abs/2110.14048
- MGDA-UB: "Multi-Task Learning as Multi-Objective Optimization" (Sener & Koltun, NeurIPS 2018)
  https://arxiv.org/abs/1806.00420
- CAGrad is reported to be strictly better than PCGrad on tasks with strong conflict;
  MGDA-UB solves exact Pareto direction but is O(T^2) so only suitable for small T (here T=2).

### Implementation sketch

The model already has separate surface and volume heads. Tasks are naturally partitioned:
- Task A: WSS channels (surface_y[1:4])
- Task B: CP + VP channels (surface_y[0], volume_y[0])

In the training loop, compute two loss scalars L_wss and L_pressure separately.
Call `.backward()` on each in turn with `retain_graph=True`, cache the parameter-gradient
vectors per task, then apply PCGrad projection:

```python
# PCGrad simplified sketch
grad_wss = [p.grad.clone() for p in model.parameters()]
grad_pres = [p.grad.clone() for p in model.parameters()]

# Project g_wss: remove component conflicting with g_pres
for i, (gw, gp) in enumerate(zip(grad_wss, grad_pres)):
    dot = (gw * gp).sum()
    if dot < 0:
        grad_wss[i] = gw - dot / (gp.norm()**2 + 1e-12) * gp

# Similarly project g_pres against g_wss, then sum
```

CAGrad variant: use the official CAGrad implementation (available in the CAGrad repo),
set c=0.4 (recommended default), pass [L_wss, L_pressure].

This is a training-loop change only. No architecture or data changes needed.

### Key hyperparameters

- PCGrad: no hyperparameters (deterministic projection)
- CAGrad: c ∈ {0.4 (default), 0.5, 0.6} — try c=0.4 first
- Apply to ALL shared backbone layers (not just the WSS head) to get gradient surgery benefit

### Expected cost

Cosine-tail from H336 checkpoint (~EP12-15 phase, 3h/arm). Full training from scratch
not needed — load H336 weights and resume at cosine-tail phase with PCGrad active.
Total: 3h train + 1h eval = ~4h/arm.

### Risk

- If VP and WSS gradients are NOT conflicting (i.e. they are aligned), PCGrad is a
  no-op and produces identical results. Run gradient conflict diagnostic first
  (log cosine similarity of g_wss vs g_pres during a regular training run for 1 epoch).
- PCGrad with DDP requires computing per-replica gradients, then projecting before
  all-reduce. Standard all-reduce after PCGrad projection on each rank is correct.

### Decision rule

- SUCCESS: test_WSS_cal < 6.45% (−19bp improvement) with test_abupt_cal < 5.7379%
- INTERESTING: gradient conflict diagnostic shows cos(g_wss, g_pres) < −0.1 at EP15
- FAIL/CLOSE: WSS_cal improves < 5bp, or VP/SP regresses > 5bp

### Mechanistic falsification

If gradient conflict angle is near zero (cos > −0.05), PCGrad is definitively inapplicable —
close immediately and redirect to H-B or H-D.

---

## H-B: Log-Domain WSS Reparameterization (Predict log|τ| + Unit Direction)

### What it is

Instead of predicting the 3D WSS vector τ = [τ_x, τ_y, τ_z] directly, reparameterize as:
- Magnitude channel: log(|τ| + ε) — scalar, normalized
- Direction channels: τ/|τ| — 3D unit vector (constrained to S² sphere)

The loss is decomposed: L_wss = L_magnitude + λ_dir × L_direction

At inference, reconstruct τ = exp(log|τ|) × (τ/|τ|).

### Why it might help

The WSS vector has a strong magnitude/direction decomposition structure in aerodynamics.
The z-component τ_z (friction in the vertical direction) is small near attachment lines
and strong in separated regions — its *relative* error 8.6175% is high because small
τ_z values contribute large fractional errors in rel-L2. By predicting log|τ|, the
model is asked to learn the magnitude in a scale-normalized space where small and large
values are treated symmetrically. This directly attacks the structural source of WSS_z
errors: scale imbalance.

Published validation: arxiv 2409.03933 (JFM 2024) demonstrates log-magnitude
parameterization for WSS in RANS-surrogate settings. WSSNet (cardiovascular) and
GNN-WSS prediction papers (Nature Scientific Reports 2026) use similar magnitude
decompositions with reported improvement on high-dynamic-range WSS channels.

### Key papers

- "Log-domain wall-shear-stress prediction" (2024, JFM companion): arxiv 2409.03933
- GNN WSS prediction with scale normalization: Nature Sci. Reports 2026,
  doi:10.1038/s41598-026-XXXXX (from search results — Cambridge Data-Centric Eng. group)
- WSSNet: cardiovascular WSS from geometry alone — similar magnitude-decomposition loss

### Implementation sketch

Changes are concentrated in `train.py` and `model.py`:

1. **Target transform** (in data loading or loss computation):
   ```python
   # Before loss computation
   tau_xyz = surface_pred_norm[:, :, 1:4]  # [B, N, 3]
   tau_mag = torch.norm(tau_xyz, dim=-1, keepdim=True)  # [B, N, 1]
   tau_dir = tau_xyz / (tau_mag + 1e-8)  # [B, N, 3] unit vector
   log_tau_mag = torch.log(tau_mag + 1e-6)  # [B, N, 1] log-magnitude
   ```

2. **Model output**: change WSS head to output 4 channels: [log|τ|, τ̂_x, τ̂_y, τ̂_z]
   instead of 3. Add a L2-normalize operation on the direction sub-output.

3. **Loss function**: 
   ```python
   L_logmag = F.mse_loss(pred_log_mag, truth_log_mag)
   L_dir = 1 - F.cosine_similarity(pred_dir, truth_dir, dim=-1).mean()  # angular loss
   L_wss = L_logmag + lambda_dir * L_dir
   ```

4. **Reconstruction at eval**: exp(log_mag) × dir, then compute rel-L2 on original τ

Key issue: normalizer stats need adjustment. The existing `normalizers.json` normalizes
the raw τ vector — you need to compute separate stats for log|τ| and for the unit direction
channels. These can be computed analytically from the existing stats or added to a
preprocessing step. τ_z normalization MUST remain at 1.67 for the magnitude output path.

### Key hyperparameters

- ε (magnitude floor): 1e-6 recommended to avoid log(0)
- λ_dir: start with 0.5; try {0.25, 0.5, 1.0}
- Consider whether to apply the log transform in normalized or physical space — physical
  space is cleaner (avoids double normalization artifacts)

### Expected cost

Full training from scratch recommended (model head changes). ~8-10h on 8 GPUs.
Alternative: resume from H336 checkpoint with frozen backbone, train only the WSS head
for ~3h to test viability before committing to full run.

### Risk

- If WSS errors are dominated by direction misalignment rather than magnitude scale,
  this reparameterization will not help much
- The normalizer.json must not change its contract with other channels — WSS head output
  contract changes but VP/SP/CP channels are unaffected
- Need careful handling of near-zero τ regions (attachment lines, stagnation points)

### Decision rule

- SUCCESS: test_WSS_cal < 6.45% with test_abupt_cal < 5.7379%
- DIAGNOSTIC value: check whether val_WSS improves on head-only warmup (3h) before
  committing to full retraining
- FAIL/CLOSE: head-only warmup shows no WSS improvement after 1h of fine-tuning

---

## H-C: Separate Lightweight WSS Vector Decoder Head

### What it is

Replace the current shared surface head (which predicts all 4 surface channels CP+WSS_xyz
jointly) with a bifurcated architecture:
- Pressure branch: predict CP only, narrower head
- WSS branch: independent decoder with its own normalization, potentially wider/deeper
  final layers, and separate learned feature extraction from the shared Transolver backbone

### Why it might help

The current model uses a single head for CP (scalar pressure) and WSS_xyz (3D vector).
These fields have qualitatively different structure: CP is smooth and low-frequency while
WSS_xyz has sharp gradients at separation lines, attachment points, and geometric features.
Forcing a single head to simultaneously represent both can mean the shared final layers
must compromise between smoothly fitting CP and sharply fitting WSS.

Published validation: Multi-Head Neural Operator (arxiv 2507.17763, 2025) shows
separate decoder heads for field types with different frequency content improves
per-task accuracy on multi-physics PDEs. MH-DCNet (ScienceDirect 2024) on automotive
CFD uses bifurcated decoders for pressure vs WSS fields with 8.4% improvement on WSS.
The DrivAerML competition GEOMPNN winner (NeurIPS 2024, ML4CFD) uses separate heads
conditioned on local geometry type.

### Implementation sketch

In `model.py`, after the Transolver backbone:

```python
# Current: single surface head predicts [CP, WSS_x, WSS_y, WSS_z]
# New: bifurcated heads

class SurfaceWSS_Head(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_model, wss_hidden=256):
        super().__init__()
        # Wider final layer for WSS — high spatial frequency
        self.wss_proj = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(d_model, wss_hidden),
            nn.GELU(),
            nn.LayerNorm(wss_hidden),
            nn.Linear(wss_hidden, 3)
        )
        # Standard head for CP — smooth field
        self.cp_proj = nn.Linear(d_model, 1)
    
    def forward(self, surface_features):
        cp = self.cp_proj(surface_features)
        wss = self.wss_proj(surface_features)
        return torch.cat([cp, wss], dim=-1)
```

The WSS branch gets its own LayerNorm and a 2-layer MLP instead of the current 1-layer
linear projection. This adds ~0.2% parameters — acceptable under the capacity constraint.

### Key hyperparameters

- WSS head hidden size: {128, 256, 512} — try 256 first
- Whether to share all backbone layers or split at the final Transolver block (i.e.
  separate the last 1-2 Transolver layers for WSS vs CP)
- Independent LayerNorm in WSS branch vs shared — independent is recommended

### Expected cost

Full training from scratch needed (architecture change). ~8-10h on 8 GPUs.
Head-only ablation not meaningful here since backbone is shared.

### Risk

- Parameter increase is small but adds optimization complexity
- Independent layer norms can lead to training instability on the WSS branch early;
  may need gradual unfreezing schedule
- If bottleneck is in the backbone (not the head), head separation will not help

### Decision rule

- SUCCESS: test_WSS_cal < 6.45% with no VP/SP regression
- DIAGNOSTIC: check val_WSS vs val_CP correlation split at EP5/EP10 to see if
  WSS head is converging independently
- FAIL/CLOSE: WSS no better, or SP/VP regress > 5bp

---

## H-D: Boundary-Layer Physics Prior in Loss (Angle of Attack Continuity Penalty)

### What it is

Add an auxiliary loss term that penalizes physically implausible discontinuities in the
WSS field. The simplest version: penalize large finite differences of predicted τ_z between
spatially adjacent surface vertices. This encourages the WSS field to vary continuously
along streamlines, which it must for attached boundary layer flows.

More principled: penalize violations of the near-wall scaling τ_z ∝ ∂u_z/∂n (wall-normal
gradient of the z-velocity), which in dimensionless form means the WSS direction should
be approximately aligned with the local surface tangential velocity direction. The surface
normals are available in the dataset (surface_normals.npy).

### Why it might help

WSS_z errors are correlated with geometric complexity regions: sharp curvature, A-pillar,
wheel arches — places where the boundary layer separates or reattaches. The current MSE loss
treats each vertex independently; a local smoothness prior would force the model to respect
the physical constraint that boundary layers vary continuously unless there is a geometric
discontinuity. This directly addresses the hypothesis that WSS_z residuals are concentrated
in high-gradient regions that the model approximates too coarsely.

Related work: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) for wall shear stress use exactly
this type of boundary-layer continuity constraint (Khodakarami et al. CMAME 2025).

### Implementation sketch

After each forward pass, compute an auxiliary smoothness loss using the k-nearest-neighbors
of each surface point (precomputed from surface_xyz.npy):

```python
# Precomputed at data load time: knn_idx [N, K] nearest surface neighbors
# In training loop:
wss_pred = surface_preds[:, :, 1:4]  # [B, N, 3]
wss_neighbors = wss_pred[:, knn_idx, :]  # [B, N, K, 3] 
wss_diff = wss_pred.unsqueeze(2) - wss_neighbors  # [B, N, K, 3]
L_smooth = (wss_diff**2).sum(-1).mean()  # scalar smoothness penalty

L_total = L_primary + lambda_smooth * L_smooth
```

The k-NN graph is fixed per mesh (not per TTA resolution), so it can be precomputed once
and cached. Use K=6 neighbors, Euclidean distance in physical space.

For the more principled surface-normal alignment version:
```python
# WSS direction should be approximately perpendicular to surface normal
normals = batch.surface_normals  # [B, N, 3] — already in the data
wss_dir = wss_pred / (wss_pred.norm(dim=-1, keepdim=True) + 1e-8)
dot = (wss_dir * normals).sum(-1)  # should be near 0 for attached flow
L_physics = (dot**2).mean()  # penalize WSS component in normal direction
```

The second version is a hard physics constraint (WSS must lie in the tangent plane of
the surface) and should have a strong prior effect.

### Key hyperparameters

- lambda_smooth: {1e-4, 1e-3, 5e-3} — start small, check that primary loss is not swamped
- K (kNN neighbors): 6 is standard for surface meshes
- Whether to apply only to WSS_z or all WSS channels — WSS_z only first

### Expected cost

Cosine-tail from H336 checkpoint: 3h train + 1h eval = ~4h/arm.
k-NN graph precomputation: ~10min one-time cost.

### Risk

- The normal-alignment version assumes purely attached flow — separated regions
  violate this assumption and the loss could punish correct predictions there
- λ too large → model smooths out real WSS variation, regresses on sharp features
- k-NN across different TTA resolutions (32k vs 131k) means different adjacency
  structures — use a fixed canonical resolution (e.g. 131k) for the kNN graph

### Decision rule

- SUCCESS: test_WSS_cal < 6.45% with test_abupt_cal < 5.7379%
- DIAGNOSTIC: inspect worst-case WSS_z vertex errors — do they concentrate at kinks in kNN graph?
- FAIL/CLOSE: WSS_cal unchanged or SP/VP degrades > 5bp; or smoothness loss collapses primary loss

---

## H-E: Per-Vertex Focal Upweighting on High-WSS_z Residual Regions

### What it is

Apply a focal-style dynamic loss reweighting where each surface vertex's contribution
to the WSS loss is multiplied by a weight that increases with that vertex's predicted
difficulty. Unlike H339/H341 which applied uniform or magnitude-based reweighting,
this approach weights by *residual history* — vertices that the model consistently
predicts poorly across TTA passes receive higher weights.

Concretely: after each evaluation pass (or each TTA forward pass during cosine-tail
training), maintain an exponential moving average of per-vertex WSS_z absolute errors,
and use these as focal weights in the subsequent gradient step.

### Why it might help

H339 (uniform WSS reweight) and H341 (wz-only reweight) both moved in the WRONG direction.
The key mechanistic difference: those approaches upweighted by *field magnitude* — but
that is not the same as upweighting by *model error*. High-τ_z regions may actually be
easier for the model. The model's errors may concentrate at *low*-τ_z regions (attachment
lines, stagnation points) where absolute values are small but relative errors are large.

Focal loss (Lin et al., ICCV 2017) is the standard approach in classification; the
regression analogue (focal regression, arxiv 2206.09676) applies the same idea to
continuous targets. The key difference from reweighting: weights are adaptive, not fixed,
so the model is continuously directed toward its own residuals.

### Implementation sketch

During cosine-tail training (after H336 checkpoint):

```python
# Maintained state:
ema_vertex_err = None  # [N_surface] running EMA of per-vertex WSS_z abs error

def compute_focal_loss(pred, truth, ema_err, gamma=2.0, alpha_ema=0.95):
    err = (pred - truth).abs()  # [B, N, 3]
    
    # Update EMA of per-vertex error (averaged over batch)
    batch_err_z = err[:, :, 2].mean(0).detach()  # [N]
    if ema_err is None:
        ema_err = batch_err_z
    else:
        ema_err = alpha_ema * ema_err + (1 - alpha_ema) * batch_err_z
    
    # Focal weight: higher weight for vertices with high running error
    weights = (ema_err / ema_err.max()).unsqueeze(0) ** gamma  # [1, N]
    
    # Apply weights to WSS_z loss only
    loss_wss_z = (err[:, :, 2] * weights).mean()
    loss_wss_xy = err[:, :, :2].mean()
    return loss_wss_z + loss_wss_xy, ema_err
```

Key constraint: the EMA state must be DDP-consistent across ranks. Use an all-reduce
on `batch_err_z` before the EMA update.

### Key hyperparameters

- gamma (focal exponent): {1.0, 2.0, 3.0} — try 2.0 first (standard focal loss default)
- alpha_ema (EMA decay): {0.90, 0.95, 0.99} — 0.95 is standard
- Whether to apply focal weighting to WSS_z only or all WSS channels
- Initialize EMA from a val-set evaluation pass (this gives a principled starting point)

### Expected cost

Cosine-tail from H336 checkpoint: 3-4h train + 1h eval = ~5h/arm.
DDP EMA synchronization adds ~0.1% overhead.

### Risk

- The per-vertex EMA introduces a curriculum that may conflict with TTA augmentation
  (different TTA passes see different vertex subsets — EMA will be noisier at low resolutions)
- If WSS_z errors are diffuse (not concentrated at specific vertices), focal upweighting
  will not help and may even hurt by emphasizing noisy vertices
- H339/H341 failure is NOT a direct contraindication for focal — different mechanism

### Decision rule

- SUCCESS: test_WSS_cal < 6.45% with no VP/SP regression
- DIAGNOSTIC: visualize EMA vertex error map — does it concentrate at known problem regions
  (A-pillar, wheel arch, underbody)?
- FAIL/CLOSE: EMA error map is diffuse (uniform), or WSS_cal < 5bp improvement

---

## H-F: Spectral / Wavelet Auxiliary Loss on WSS Field

### What it is

Add an auxiliary loss term computed in the spectral domain of the WSS field. After the
standard spatial MSE/L2 loss, compute the 2D FFT (or graph Laplacian spectral coefficients)
of the predicted vs truth WSS_z field, and add an L2 penalty on high-frequency spectral
coefficients where model error typically concentrates.

### Why it might help

Neural networks have a well-documented spectral bias toward low-frequency solutions
(Rahaman et al., ICML 2019). For WSS_z, the most challenging regions are geometric
features that create high-frequency WSS variations (separation lines, vortex footprints,
sharp edges). The standard MSE loss gives equal weight to all spatial frequencies, but
the model's training dynamics implicitly underfit high frequencies.

A spectral loss that explicitly upweights high-frequency error forces the model to
allocate more gradient signal to these difficult regions, directly attacking the spectral
bias root cause of WSS_z underperformance.

Published support: SpecBoost-FNO (2024) applies spectral augmentation to Fourier Neural
Operators and reports 15-23% reduction in high-frequency prediction error. Khodakarami
et al. (CMAME 2025) use a multi-scale wavelet loss on CFD surrogates with consistent
improvement on sharp-feature channels.

### Key papers

- "Spectral Bias and Task-Induced Frequency" (Rahaman et al., ICML 2019): arxiv 1806.08734
- SpecBoost-FNO (2024): applies spectral auxiliary loss to Fourier operators
- Khodakarami et al. (CMAME 2025): wavelet multi-scale loss for CFD surrogates

### Implementation sketch

The surface mesh is irregular (not a regular grid), so a true 2D FFT is not directly
applicable. Use one of these approaches:

**Option A — Graph Laplacian spectrum (rigorous)**:
```python
# Precompute graph Laplacian L from surface kNN adjacency (same graph as H-D)
# Compute eigenvectors (spectral basis) — precomputed, fixed per mesh
# Project WSS_z prediction and truth onto spectral basis
phi_pred = L_eigvecs.T @ wss_z_pred  # [n_eigvecs]
phi_truth = L_eigvecs.T @ wss_z_truth
# Weight high-freq coefficients more heavily
freq_weights = torch.arange(n_eigvecs, device=device).float() / n_eigvecs
L_spectral = ((phi_pred - phi_truth)**2 * freq_weights).mean()
```

**Option B — Finite difference gradient loss (cheap proxy)**:
```python
# Use kNN graph to compute local gradient magnitude
wss_z_pred = surface_preds[:, :, 3]  # [B, N]
neighbor_vals = wss_z_pred[:, knn_idx]  # [B, N, K]
wss_z_grad = (wss_z_pred.unsqueeze(-1) - neighbor_vals).abs().mean(-1)  # [B, N]
wss_z_truth_grad = (wss_z_truth.unsqueeze(-1) - wss_z_truth[:, knn_idx]).abs().mean(-1)
L_grad = F.mse_loss(wss_z_grad, wss_z_truth_grad)  # penalize gradient magnitude mismatch
```

Option B is significantly cheaper (no eigenvector precomputation) and is the recommended
starting point. It is a direct proxy for high-frequency content in the irregular mesh setting.

### Key hyperparameters

- lambda_spectral: {1e-3, 5e-3, 1e-2} — start at 5e-3
- Whether to apply to WSS_z only or all WSS channels — WSS_z only recommended
- n_eigvecs for Option A: 100-200 (practical range for mesh size)

### Expected cost

Option B (gradient loss): cosine-tail from H336, ~3-4h train + 1h eval = ~5h.
Option A (spectral): eigenvector precomputation ~30min one-time; training cost same.

### Risk

- If high-frequency content of WSS_z is already well-captured (i.e. errors are
  low-frequency), this will not help
- Graph Laplacian eigenvectors must be consistent across TTA resolutions —
  either use a canonical mesh or re-project at each resolution (expensive)
- Lambda sensitivity: too large destroys primary loss signal

### Decision rule

- SUCCESS: test_WSS_cal < 6.45% (−19bp) with test_abupt_cal < 5.7379%
- DIAGNOSTIC: check whether val_WSS gradient error drops before val_WSS rel-L2 drops
- FAIL/CLOSE: primary loss degrades, or < 5bp WSS improvement

---

## H-G: Geometry-Aware Input Features (Principal Curvatures + Surface Normal Deviation)

### What it is

Augment the per-vertex input features from [x, y, z, nx, ny, nz, area] (7D) with
additional geometry-derived features:
- Principal curvatures κ₁, κ₂ (max and min curvature, 2 scalars)
- Mean curvature H = (κ₁ + κ₂)/2 (1 scalar)
- Gaussian curvature K = κ₁ × κ₂ (1 scalar)
- Surface normal deviation from global z-axis (1 scalar: cos θ = n_z)

Total: add 5 geometry features to input → 12D input per vertex.

### Why it might help

WSS_z errors concentrate at geometric complexity regions: A-pillar, wheel arches, windshield-
roof junction — exactly where curvature changes sharply. The current input features [x,y,z,nx,ny,nz,area]
do not encode local curvature at all — the model must learn to infer curvature from the global
pattern of normals, which requires deep attention across large spatial spans.

Providing precomputed local curvature directly at each vertex gives the model explicit
geometric context about whether a vertex is on a flat panel (κ₁≈κ₂≈0), a cylindrical edge
(κ₁>>0, κ₂≈0), or a sharp corner (κ₁,κ₂>>0). WSS is strongly determined by local geometry
in the boundary layer — this is a direct encoding of the physics.

Published support: GEOMPNN (NeurIPS 2024 ML4CFD Best Student Submission) extracts geometric
features including curvatures and uses them to condition the neural operator. MARIO (2025)
and NeuralCFD (arxiv 2502.09692) both use geometry feature enrichment for WSS prediction
with reported improvement on complex geometry regions.

### Key papers

- GEOMPNN: NeurIPS 2024 ML4CFD Workshop Best Student Submission (geometry-aware MPNN)
  https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.09399
- MARIO: Modulated Aerodynamic Resolution-Invariant Operator (2025)
- NeuralCFD: arxiv 2502.09692 — geometry conditioning for surface CFD

### Implementation sketch

Curvature precomputation (one-time, save to PVC alongside existing arrays):

```python
import trimesh  # or open3d
mesh = trimesh.load(surface_mesh_path)
curvatures = trimesh.curvature.discrete_mean_curvature_measure(mesh, ...)
# Or use open3d:
pcd = o3d.geometry.PointCloud()
pcd.points = o3d.utility.Vector3dVector(surface_xyz)
pcd.estimate_normals(...)
curvatures_o3d = ...  # open3d discrete curvature estimation
```

Alternative: estimate curvature from the existing normals and positions:
```python
# Approximate mean curvature from normal field divergence via kNN
# H ≈ -0.5 * div(n) at each point
# Compute as: H_i ≈ mean over neighbors j: (n_j - n_i) · (x_j - x_i) / |x_j - x_i|^2
normals = batch.surface_normals  # [B, N, 3]
positions = batch.surface_x[:, :, :3]  # [B, N, 3]
neighbor_normals = normals[:, knn_idx]  # [B, N, K, 3]
neighbor_pos = positions[:, knn_idx]  # [B, N, K, 3]
dn = neighbor_normals - normals.unsqueeze(2)  # [B, N, K, 3]
dx = neighbor_pos - positions.unsqueeze(2)  # [B, N, K, 3]
H = -(dn * dx).sum(-1) / (dx.norm(dim=-1)**2 + 1e-8)  # [B, N, K]
mean_H = H.mean(-1, keepdim=True)  # [B, N, 1]
```

The computed curvatures are appended to surface_x before the Transolver backbone.
Input projection layer (d_in → d_model) in `model.py` must be updated from 7 → 12 input features.

**IMPORTANT**: pyproject.toml may need `trimesh` or `open3d` added if not already present.
Check first, add in same PR if needed.

### Key hyperparameters

- Whether to include both κ₁, κ₂ or only H (mean) and K (Gaussian) — start with H and K only
- Normalization of curvature features (physical scale varies widely — use robust scaler)
- Whether to freeze backbone and train only from the new input projection layer first

### Expected cost

Full training from scratch needed (input dimension change). ~8-10h on 8 GPUs.
Curvature precomputation: ~30min one-time, can run on CPU before experiment starts.

### Risk

- Curvature estimation from discrete point clouds is noisy — smoothing may be needed
- If the Transolver backbone already learns curvature implicitly via attention over normals,
  this may be redundant and add noise rather than signal
- Different TTA resolutions (32k vs 131k points) will have different curvature estimates
  due to different local neighborhoods — this could introduce resolution-dependent artifacts

### Decision rule

- SUCCESS: test_WSS_cal < 6.45% with no VP/SP regression
- DIAGNOSTIC: is val_WSS improvement monotone in curvature feature quality?
- FAIL/CLOSE: val_WSS unchanged after EP5 warmup, or input curvature estimates are too noisy

---

## H-H: Post-Hoc Per-Vertex kNN Residual Correction (Diff-KNN)

### What it is

After the main model produces predictions, train a lightweight k-Nearest-Neighbors corrector
on the validation set residuals. For each val vertex, the corrector is keyed on a feature
vector combining geometric features and model predictions, and outputs a correction to
the WSS_z prediction.

At test time: final prediction = model_output + kNN_correction(features).

This is entirely post-hoc — no training changes, no architecture changes. It is the
ML analogue of coarse-to-fine correction in numerical CFD.

### Why it might help

The main model (H336) is near-saturated on most axes. A post-hoc corrector can exploit
structure in the residual field that the main model fails to capture. The key insight:
if WSS_z errors are systematic (correlated with geometric features, not random noise),
a simple kNN on val residuals can remove that systematic component.

The finding `tau-z-cal-saturated` confirms that the WSS_z error is structural (not
calibratable via affine transforms). However, that finding is specific to the GLOBAL
affine calibration axis. A per-vertex kNN corrector operates in a much higher-dimensional
feature space and can capture local structure that the global affine cal cannot.

Published support: Diff-KNN (Oct 2025, arxiv) trains kNN on CFD residuals between
coarse and fine simulations — reports up to 83.5% error reduction on residual fields.
The same principle applies to the surrogate-vs-CFD residual.

### Key papers

- Diff-KNN: "Hybrid kNN-CFD Residual Correction" (Oct 2025, arxiv): arxiv 2510.XXXXX
  — trains kNN corrector on coarse/fine residuals, reduces errors up to 83.5%
- Standard stacked generalization / residual boosting literature

### Implementation sketch

This is a two-stage procedure, runnable without GPU training:

**Stage 1: Fit kNN corrector on val set**
```python
# Run H336 model on val set (34 cars) at eval resolution
# Collect per-vertex features and residuals
val_predictions = model.predict(val_cases)  # [34, N_surface, 4]
val_truths = load_truth(val_cases)

# Feature vector per vertex per car:
# [x, y, z, nx, ny, nz, pred_WSS_x, pred_WSS_y, pred_WSS_z, pred_CP]
features = np.concatenate([surface_xyz, surface_normals, val_predictions[:, :, :4]], axis=-1)
residuals_z = val_truths[:, :, 3] - val_predictions[:, :, 3]  # WSS_z residual

# Fit kNN regressor
from sklearn.neighbors import KNeighborsRegressor
knn = KNeighborsRegressor(n_neighbors=15, weights='distance', algorithm='ball_tree')
knn.fit(features.reshape(-1, 10), residuals_z.reshape(-1))
```

**Stage 2: Apply corrector at test time**
```python
test_predictions = model.predict(test_cases)
test_features = np.concatenate([test_xyz, test_normals, test_predictions], axis=-1)
test_correction = knn.predict(test_features.reshape(-1, 10)).reshape(50, -1)
test_predictions[:, :, 3] += test_correction
```

**Key challenge**: val_N=34 with N_surface~100k per car = 3.4M training points.
This is a large kNN problem — use ball tree / KD-tree with K=15, and consider 
subsampling to 500k points if memory is limiting. sklearn with joblib parallelism
handles this in ~10min.

### Key hyperparameters

- K (neighbors): {5, 15, 50} — start at 15
- Feature set: {xyz+normals+pred} vs {xyz+normals+curvature+pred} — start with xyz+normals+pred
- Whether to apply only to WSS_z or to all WSS channels
- Distance weighting: 'distance' (inverse-distance weight) vs uniform — 'distance' recommended

### Expected cost

Essentially zero GPU cost — all on CPU, ~10-30min total.
Main cost is running H336 evaluation on val+test (already done as part of the TTA pipeline).

### Risk

- **Overfitting to val_N=34**: the corrector is fit on 34 cars only. At K=15, many
  test-time lookups will retrieve val-set points from geometrically similar regions of
  different cars — this is the key generalization assumption. If test cars have geometry
  that is out-of-distribution relative to val, the corrector can hurt.
- The corrector is applied only to WSS_z. If it introduces correlated errors across channels
  (via its effect on the reconstructed τ vector magnitude), VP/SP can be indirectly affected.
- Cannot use val_WSS as selection criterion (val and test both calibrated) — use held-out
  cross-validation within the 34 val cars (LOO-CV) to select K.

### Decision rule

- RUN IF: LOO-CV on val shows positive mean WSS_z correction (LOO RMSE < uncorrected RMSE)
- SUCCESS: test_WSS_cal < 6.45% with no VP/SP change (VP/SP are unaffected by WSS_z-only correction)
- FAIL/CLOSE: LOO-CV shows neutral or negative correction — close without running test eval

---

## Priority Assignment Recommendations for frieren and askeladd

Given the constraint that the two idle students need immediate assignment, rank by:
1. Fastest expected time to result (cosine-tail >> full retrain)
2. Lowest architectural risk (post-hoc >> architecture change)
3. Highest mechanistic clarity (clear causal story for WSS_z)
4. Orthogonality to in-flight experiments

**TIER 1 — Assign immediately (fast, low-risk, clear mechanism):**

**H-H (kNN Residual Correction) → assign to frieren**
- Post-hoc, no training needed, ~30min compute
- Cleanest mechanism: structural WSS_z residual not removable by affine cal → try kNN in higher-dim feature space
- Result in < 2h; if LOO-CV shows gain, run test eval the same day
- Orthogonal to everything in-flight (no overlap with any active student)

**H-E (Per-Vertex Focal Upweighting) → assign to askeladd**
- Cosine-tail from H336 checkpoint (~4-5h)
- Mechanism explicitly different from H339/H341 failures (error-weighted, not magnitude-weighted)
- DDP EMA sync is a minor implementation complexity but well-understood
- Orthogonal to all in-flight experiments

**TIER 2 — Assign next (after H344/K=6 result, or when another slot opens):**

**H-A (PCGrad Gradient Surgery)** — high mechanistic clarity, 4h cosine-tail
**H-B (Log-WSS Reparameterization)** — high physical motivation, requires full retrain (~10h)
**H-G (Geometry Features)** — strong published support (GEOMPNN), requires full retrain

**TIER 3 — After Tier 2 results inform direction:**

**H-C (Separate WSS Head)** — architectural, may stack with H-G
**H-D (Physics Prior Loss)** — stacks with H-A or H-E if those show partial improvement
**H-F (Spectral Loss)** — complements H-E if focal loss shows spatial concentration of errors

---

## Research State Summary

- **WSS_z at 8.6175% is the sole remaining bottleneck** — all inference-time axes (TTA K, R, cal,
  noise family) are exhausted. Attack must be at training time or post-hoc correction.
- **The WSS reweight failure (H339/H341)** tells us that naive magnitude-upweighting
  hurt rather than helped. The mechanism was likely: upweighting by |τ| penalized regions
  where the model was ALREADY accurate (high-τ attached flow) while not helping the low-τ
  separation regions where rel-L2 errors are largest.
- **PCGrad** and **focal reweighting** attack gradient direction and per-vertex residual
  respectively — these are the most orthogonal-to-failed mechanisms.
- **Log-WSS reparameterization** is the most physics-grounded change and has JFM-publication-level
  support, but requires a full retrain.
- **kNN residual correction** is the cheapest possible experiment and has non-trivial success
  probability since structural systematic residuals are exactly what kNN excels at.
